ic_001.text: The freight elevator groans as it descends into sub-level nine of the Foundry District. The air thickens with heat and the smell of molten slag. When the cage door rattles open, you step into a cathedral of industry — blast furnaces tall as buildings, rivers of liquid metal flowing through channels cut into the stone floor. Hundreds of workers move through the orange haze, their bodies wrapped in heat-resistant suits that do almost nothing. This is where Meridian's memory stacks begin life, as raw ore pulled from the earth by people who will never afford the finished product.

choices.text: Look for the contact you were told about
Watch the workers for a while

ic_002.text: You find her at Furnace Seven — a broad-shouldered woman with burn scars running up both arms and eyes that have seen too many twelve-hour shifts. She's called Rivka. She doesn't look at you when she speaks. 'You're the one Marlo sent? You don't look like much.' She spits into the furnace channel. 'But then, none of us did before we woke up. Follow me. Kael wants to see you himself.'

choices.text: Follow Rivka without questions
"Who's Kael?"
"I'm not here to join anything. I just need information."

ic_003.text: You lean against a support column and watch. The workers move with the mechanical efficiency of people who've done the same motions ten thousand times. A young boy — can't be older than thirteen — hauls a cart of cooling molds past you, his hands wrapped in rags already soaked through with sweat. An overseer drone hovers near the ceiling, its red eye scanning for anyone moving too slowly. One woman stumbles, and the drone emits a sharp warning tone. She straightens immediately, terror flashing across her face before her expression goes blank again.

choices.text: Approach the woman who stumbled
Find your contact

ic_004.text: Rivka leads you through a labyrinth of maintenance tunnels behind the main foundry floor. The walls are lined with pipes that hiss and leak steam. She stops at a blast door with a crude symbol scratched into the metal — a fist gripping a gear. She raps a pattern: three-two-one. The door opens from inside, revealing a space that was once a pump station, now converted into something between a war room and a chapel. Maps cover the walls. Crates of supplies line the floor. And at the center, standing over a table covered in blueprints, is the man himself.

choices.text: Study Kael before he notices you
Step forward and introduce yourself

ic_005.text: Rivka's expression shifts — not quite respect, but something close. 'Kael Ironfist. Used to be shift foreman on Level Twelve until he watched three workers die in a coolant breach that Meridian knew about and refused to fix. He organized the first walkout this district has seen in thirty years. Sec-Corp put a bounty on his head. He's been down here ever since, building something bigger than a walkout.' She pauses. 'He's building a revolution.'

choices.text: "Take me to him."
"A revolution against Meridian? That's suicide."

ic_006.text: Rivka finally looks at you. Her eyes are hard. 'Information. That's what everyone says at first. Then they see a child lose three fingers to a press because the safety guards were removed to increase output by two percent. Then they see a friend dragged out by Sec-Corp for talking about unions. Then information isn't enough anymore.' She turns back to the furnace. 'You can leave the way you came. Or you can follow me and see what we're actually doing about it. Your choice.'

choices.text: Follow her
Leave the foundry

ic_007.text: You approach carefully. She's older than you first thought, maybe fifty, but the foundry has a way of aging people in dog years. Her name tag reads ELENA. When you get close, she flinches. 'Don't,' she whispers without moving her lips. 'The drone will flag us both for unscheduled interaction.' But her eyes dart to her left, toward a maintenance corridor. 'Shift break in four minutes. Tunnel Three.'

choices.text: Wait for the shift break and meet her at Tunnel Three
Nod and go find your contact instead

ic_008.text: Kael Ironfist is not what you expected. He's tall, yes, built like a man who's spent twenty years hauling steel. His forearms are scarred with burn marks that form almost deliberate patterns. But it's his face that catches you — calm, thoughtful, the face of someone who reads books between shifts. His left hand is cybernetic, a crude industrial prosthetic with exposed servos, clearly self-installed. He marks something on a blueprint with precise, careful strokes.

choices.text: Wait for him to acknowledge you
Clear your throat

ic_009.text: Kael looks up. His eyes — one natural brown, one replaced with a salvaged optic that glows faint amber — settle on you with unsettling focus. 'So you're the one from topside.' His voice is deep, measured, the voice of a man who learned long ago that shouting wastes energy. 'Rivka says you might be useful. I say everyone's useful if they're willing to bleed for the right cause.' He pushes a blueprint toward you. It shows the layout of a Meridian supply depot. 'We need medical supplies. The company store prices them at two days' wages for a bandage. We can take what the workers need, or we can keep watching them die on the line. What do you say?'

choices.text: "I'm in. Tell me the plan."
"Stealing from Meridian will bring Sec-Corp down on this whole district."
"What's in it for me?"

ic_010.text: Four minutes later, the shift horn blows. You slip into Tunnel Three and find Elena already there, leaning against the pipes, hands shaking as she gulps water from a dented canteen. 'You're not a worker,' she says. 'I can tell. You still have hope in your eyes.' She tells you about the last three months — accelerated quotas, removed safety equipment, two deaths covered up as 'equipment malfunctions.' She tells you about whispers of a man organizing in the sub-levels. 'They call him Ironfist. If you find him, tell him Elena from Furnace Three is ready.'

choices.text: "I'll find him. Stay safe."
"Come with me now."

ic_011.text: Kael's expression doesn't change, but something shifts in his posture — a release of tension you hadn't noticed he was holding. 'Good. Here's what we know.' He taps the blueprint. 'Meridian Supply Depot 14-C. Two blocks east, sub-level six. Lightly guarded — four Sec-Corp officers on rotation, two combat drones, one automated sentry at the loading bay. Medical supplies are in Section B. In and out in twelve minutes or the district patrol responds.' He looks at Rivka. 'Take two others. Our new friend leads. Consider it an audition.'

choices.text: "I work alone."
"Who else is on the team?"
Study the blueprint carefully before heading out

ic_012.text: Kael nods slowly. 'You're right. It will. And then what? Sec-Corp is already here. They're already killing us, just slowly enough that nobody calls it murder. They price medicine so high that workers choose between healing and eating. That's not peace — that's a slaughter with paperwork.' He leans forward. 'I'm not asking you to start a war. I'm asking you to help us survive one that's already happening.'

choices.text: "When you put it that way... I'm in."
"There has to be a legal way to change things."
"I need time to think about this."

ic_013.text: The room goes quiet. Rivka's hand drifts toward the wrench at her belt. Kael raises a finger and she stops. 'What's in it for you,' he repeats, tasting the words like something bitter. 'Credits, if that's what moves you. We take from the company, and there's always surplus to sell. But I'll tell you what's really in it for you — a chance to be something other than another body the system chews up and spits into a recycler. Everyone in this room has lost someone to Meridian's bottom line. Your turn is coming whether you join us or not. At least with us, you get to hit back.'

choices.text: "Fair enough. I'll do the job."
"Credits work fine. Show me the plan."
Walk away

ic_014.text: Elena shakes her head. 'I can't. I leave now, the drone logs me as absent. They dock my pay, flag me for review. I have a daughter in the creche on Level Four.' Her voice cracks. 'But I can do things from inside. Pass information. Leave doors unlocked. Whatever your people need.' She grips your arm with surprising strength. 'Just promise me this ends. Promise me my girl grows up in something better than this.'

choices.text: "I promise."
"I can't promise that. But I can try."

ic_015.text: Kael shakes his head. 'Nobody works alone in the Collective. That's the whole point. We rise together or we fall alone — and falling alone is exactly what Meridian wants.' He crosses his arms, the servos in his prosthetic hand whining softly. 'You take the team or you don't take the job. Non-negotiable.'

choices.text: "Fine. Who's coming with me?"
"Then I guess I don't take the job."

ic_016.text: Rivka whistles, and two figures emerge from behind the supply crates. The first is a wiry man with nervous eyes and fingers that never stop moving — Splicer, the team's tech specialist, a former Meridian network technician who walked off the job after finding his own termination order in the company database. The second is Bray, a towering figure of muscle and metal, an ex-pit fighter whose left arm is a pneumatic hammer originally designed for demolition work. He nods at you once, the gesture carrying more weight than most speeches.

choices.text: "Let's move. We're burning moonlight."
Brief the team on approach strategy
Ask Splicer about the depot's security system

ic_017.text: You study the blueprint with trained eyes. The depot's ventilation system connects to the main foundry network — meaning there's an access shaft on the east wall that bypasses the front sentry entirely. The patrol routes show a thirty-second window when Section B's corridor is unwatched. And the medical supply crates are standardized Meridian containers — heavy, but they have magnetic clamps that a strong person could use to slide them along the floor channels. You've found a clean approach route that avoids the main security.

choices.text: Share the plan with the team and move out

ic_018.text: Something flickers across Kael's face — not anger, but a deep, exhausted sorrow. 'Legal.' He pulls up his sleeve. Beneath the burn scars, you can see a barcode tattooed on his forearm — a worker registration number. 'I filed seventeen grievances through legal channels. I petitioned the district labor board. I wrote to the Sector Council. You know what happened? The labor board is funded by Meridian. The Sector Council is appointed by Meridian. The law is written by Meridian. There is no legal. There's only what we take.'

choices.text: "Then let's take it. I'm with you."
"I understand, but violence creates more violence."

ic_019.text: Kael studies you for a long moment, then nods. 'The door's open when you're ready. It'll stay open. We don't force anyone.' He turns back to his blueprints. As Rivka escorts you out through the maintenance tunnels, she presses something into your hand — a small iron gear, crudely stamped with the Collective's fist symbol. 'Show that at Furnace Seven when you change your mind. Not if. When.'

choices.text: Return to the Collective later
Leave the Foundry District

ic_020.text: The four of you move through the sub-level tunnels in single file. Splicer takes point, his fingers dancing over a handheld scanner that maps the security net ahead. Bray brings up the rear, his hammer-arm folded tight against his body to fit through the narrow passages. Rivka walks beside you, silent and sure-footed. The tunnel opens into a storm drain that runs beneath the depot. Above you, through a grate, you can hear the hum of the sentry drone and the boots of a guard on patrol.

choices.text: Wait for the patrol to pass, then breach through the grate
Have Splicer disable the sentry remotely
Send Bray through first as a distraction

ic_021.text: Splicer's eyes light up — this is his territory. 'Standard Meridian Sec-Net, version eight-point-two. I helped install half these systems before they tried to have me killed.' His fingers trace invisible circuits in the air. 'Two drone patrols on a ninety-second cycle. Pressure sensors on the main doors — but not the ventilation access, because some genius in engineering decided airflow was more important than security. Camera coverage has a blind spot in the southeast corner where two units overlap and cancel each other's feed. Classic install error. I reported it three times. They never fixed it.'

choices.text: "Use the ventilation access. That's our way in."
"The camera blind spot. Can you widen it?"
"Let's just go. We're overthinking this."

ic_022.text: You lay out the ventilation approach and watch the team's reactions. Splicer grins — he'd been thinking the same thing. Bray frowns at the narrow shaft dimensions but grunts agreement. Rivka nods once, the highest praise she seems capable of. The team moves through the sub-level tunnels with purpose, arriving at the east wall ventilation access in seven minutes. Splicer pops the grate with a multitool, and cool recycled air washes over you from inside the depot.

choices.text: Enter the shaft first
Send Splicer in first to scout

ic_023.text: You time the patrol. Sixty seconds between passes. Rivka counts under her breath while Splicer works the grate bolts with a magnetic wrench that makes no sound. When the boots fade, you're through the grate and into a loading bay in under eight seconds. The depot is quiet — rows of crates under fluorescent lights that flicker at intervals. Section B is marked with a yellow stripe on the floor, thirty meters ahead. Between you and it: one camera, one motion sensor, and a whole lot of open space.

choices.text: Move fast — sprint to Section B before the camera cycles
Have Splicer loop the camera feed
Create a distraction to draw the guard

ic_024.text: Splicer jacks into a maintenance panel and works his magic. The sentry drone overhead stutters, its red eye flickering to yellow, then going dark. 'Thirty seconds before it reboots,' Splicer hisses. You move fast, the team flowing up through the grate like water. You're inside the depot loading bay with twelve seconds to spare. The sentry powers back online above you, scanning an empty drain where four people stood moments ago.

choices.text: Push toward Section B

ic_025.text: Bray looks at you, then at Rivka, then shrugs. He rips the grate off with his pneumatic arm and heaves himself up into the loading bay. The sentry drone immediately locks on, its warning klaxon splitting the air. Bray roars and charges at the nearest camera, smashing it with a backhand that sends sparks showering across the floor. Two guards come running. In the chaos, you, Rivka, and Splicer slip through unnoticed — but the clock is ticking faster now.

choices.text: Rush to Section B while Bray draws fire
Help Bray take down the guards

ic_026.text: Splicer stares at you with new respect. 'Widen the blind spot? That's...' He pulls out a device that looks like a modified radio. 'If I broadcast a calibration signal on the right frequency, both cameras will try to auto-correct simultaneously. They'll create a dead zone about six meters wide for roughly forty-five seconds. That's enough to walk through the front door.' He grins. 'I like how you think.'

choices.text: "Do it. We walk in through the blind spot."
"Good backup plan. But let's use the vents as primary."

ic_027.text: You crawl through the ventilation shaft, the metal groaning softly under your weight. Through the grate slats below, you can see the depot interior — organized rows of supply crates, the yellow Section B markings, a guard leaning against a wall checking his comm-pad. The shaft branches ahead: left toward Section B, right toward what looks like a control room.

choices.text: Go left toward Section B and the medical supplies
Go right toward the control room

ic_028.text: Splicer moves through the shaft like a spider, barely making a sound. He reaches the junction and peers through both grates, then signals back with hand signs: two fingers left, one finger right, closed fist. Two guards left path, one in the control room, all clear otherwise. He waves you forward.

choices.text: Take the left path toward Section B
Take the right path to neutralize the control room first

ic_029.text: You launch yourself across the open floor, boots barely touching the concrete. The camera is mid-sweep, panning left — you're a blur in its peripheral zone, too fast for the motion-tracking algorithm to lock on. You slide behind a crate stack in Section B, heart hammering. Safe. Rivka appears behind you moments later, followed by Splicer. Bray squeezes between the crate rows, metal arm scraping the containers. You're in.

choices.text: Start loading medical supplies

ic_030.text: You reach Section B. The medical supplies are exactly where the blueprint showed — three crates marked with Meridian's healthcare division logo, each one containing enough antibiotics, wound sealant, and pain blockers to run a field clinic for a month. Street value: more than most foundry workers make in a year. To the workers who need them: priceless. Rivka is already popping the magnetic clamps.

choices.text: Help load the supplies and get out clean
Check the other crates while you're here
Take only what's needed — leave the rest

ic_031.text: You grab a loose bolt from the floor and hurl it at a stack of empty containers across the bay. The crash echoes through the depot. The guard snaps to attention and moves to investigate, weapon drawn. The motion sensor tracks him instead of you. You have a window.

choices.text: Move to Section B while the guard investigates
Ambush the guard from behind

ic_032.text: Alarms are wailing now. Red emergency lights strobe across the depot floor. You run, Rivka and Splicer flanking you. Section B is right there — you grab the first medical crate and Rivka grabs the second. Splicer gets the third. Behind you, Bray's laughter echoes between the crashes of his fists against Sec-Corp armor. You have maybe ninety seconds before reinforcements arrive.

choices.text: Get out through the loading bay — fastest route
Go back for Bray first

ic_033.text: You vault over a crate and catch the first guard with a flying tackle that sends his stun-baton skittering across the floor. Bray has the second guard pinned against the wall with his hammer-arm, the pneumatic pistons hissing inches from the man's helmet. Both guards are down in seconds. Bray grins at you, blood trickling from a cut above his eye. 'Not bad, topside. Not bad at all.'

choices.text: "Tie them up. We've still got a job to do."

ic_034.text: Splicer broadcasts the calibration signal. Both cameras twitch, recalibrate, and leave a six-meter corridor of dead space running straight through the depot's front entrance. You walk in like you own the place. The guard at the front desk doesn't even look up from his comm-pad as you pass through his blind spot. Section B is forty seconds away.

choices.text: Move to Section B and grab the medical supplies

ic_035.text: The control room is small — one technician monitoring feeds from eight cameras. He's half-asleep, a cup of cold synth-coffee at his elbow. From here you could shut down every sensor in the depot, giving your team free run of the entire facility.

choices.text: Knock out the technician quietly
Have Splicer hack the console remotely through the vent

ic_036.text: You work fast, sliding the heavy medical crates along the floor channels. Each one weighs as much as a person, but the magnetic clamps make them glide. Eight minutes in. Four minutes to go. Rivka has the escape route mapped — back through the storm drain, then a two-kilometer crawl through maintenance tunnels to the Collective hideout. As you load the last crate, Splicer freezes. 'Movement. Two more guards coming from Section A. They're early.'

choices.text: "Move now. We've got what we came for."
Set up an ambush
Hide and let them pass

ic_037.text: The other crates in Section B contain more than medical supplies. One is marked EXPERIMENTAL — LEVEL 5 CLEARANCE REQUIRED. You pop the seal and inside, nestled in foam, are six memory stack prototypes. Not the consumer models that cost a lifetime's wages. These are military-grade, designed for consciousness transfer in field conditions. Each one could change someone's life — or sell for enough credits to fund the Collective for a year.

choices.text: Take the stack prototypes
Leave them — too risky, too traceable
Take one for study, leave the rest

ic_038.text: Rivka gives you a look. 'We're already committing a felony. Might as well stock up.' But you shake your head. You take one crate — enough for the most urgent cases. The others stay. Rivka clearly disagrees but says nothing. Sometimes restraint sends its own message.

choices.text: Head for the exit with the single crate

ic_039.text: You move up behind the guard on silent feet. One arm around his neck, controlled pressure on the carotid. He struggles for three seconds, then goes limp. You lower him behind the containers and take his keycard. Rivka materializes beside you and zip-ties his wrists with practiced efficiency. 'Clean,' she says. Coming from her, it's practically a standing ovation.

choices.text: Use his keycard on Section B

ic_040.text: You run. The crates bounce and scrape against the floor as you haul them toward the loading bay. Behind you, Bray's hammer rings against something metallic — a drone, judging by the shower of sparks. He's buying you time. The loading bay door is twenty meters away. Fifteen. Ten. A stun bolt sizzles past your ear. Five meters. You're through. Down the drain. Into the dark. Running.

choices.text: Keep running — don't look back

ic_041.text: You drop your crate and turn back. Bray is surrounded — three guards and a combat drone. His hammer arm swings in wide arcs, keeping them at bay, but a stun bolt catches him in the shoulder and his left side goes rigid. You charge in, grabbing a fallen guard's shock-baton. Together, you fight your way clear. Bray leans on you as you stagger toward the exit, his massive weight nearly buckling your knees. 'You came back,' he says, sounding surprised. 'Nobody comes back.'

choices.text: Get Bray out through the loading bay

ic_042.text: You drop through the vent grate, landing cat-quiet behind the technician's chair. Your arm snakes around his neck before he can react. He slumps forward onto his console, coffee cup tipping over in a brown puddle. You're at the controls. Every camera, every sensor, every lock in the depot is at your fingertips.

choices.text: Shut down all security and signal the team
Download the security protocols for future use

ic_043.text: Splicer works through the vent grate, his fingers flying over a handheld interface. 'Meridian really should update their firmware,' he mutters. The camera feeds freeze one by one. The motion sensors power down. The door locks click open throughout the depot. The technician doesn't notice — his screens still show the looped footage Splicer fed into the system. 'We've got eight minutes before the system runs its integrity check.'

choices.text: Move to Section B with full access

ic_044.text: You move for the exit, dragging crates behind you. Splicer triggers a smoke canister at the junction to cover your retreat. The guards' shouts fade as you descend into the storm drain, the cold water swallowing the sound of your boots. Two kilometers of darkness later, you're back at the Collective hideout, soaked, exhausted, and carrying enough medicine to save lives.

choices.text: Report to Kael

ic_045.text: You position Bray behind a crate stack and have Splicer kill the lights in Section B. When the guards round the corner, Bray's hammer catches the first one in the chest plate, sending him flying. You take the second with a boot to the knee and an elbow to the helmet. Six seconds, two guards down, no alarms triggered. Rivka doesn't even stop loading crates.

choices.text: Finish loading and get out

ic_046.text: You press yourself flat behind a crate row, barely breathing. The guards pass within arm's reach, their flashlights sweeping the floor. One of them pauses — his light lingers on a scuff mark your boot left on the concrete. Your heart stops. Then his partner calls him forward and he moves on. You wait thirty seconds, then resume loading.

choices.text: Finish the job and get out

ic_047.text: You stuff the prototypes into your pack. They're heavier than they look — each one a dense cylinder of compressed neural circuitry worth more than the depot itself. Rivka watches you with wide eyes. 'Do you know what those are?' She does. Everyone in the Foundry District knows what memory stacks look like. The difference is that these are usually behind three feet of vault steel, not sitting in a supply depot. Something about this feels wrong, but the potential is undeniable.

choices.text: "We'll figure out what to do with them later. Move."

ic_048.text: You take a single prototype, slipping it into your jacket's inner pocket. It sits against your chest like a second heartbeat, warm from its own internal power source. One is enough to study, to understand. The rest you leave — if Meridian discovers the theft, one missing unit might go unnoticed in inventory. Six would trigger a full investigation.

choices.text: Get back to loading the medical supplies

ic_049.text: The hideout erupts when you arrive. Workers crowd around the medical crates, some of them crying. A woman unwraps a wound sealant and presses it to a burn on her son's arm that's been festering for weeks. An old man clutches a bottle of antibiotics like a holy relic. Kael stands apart, watching. When the crowd thins, he approaches you. 'You did well. The workers will eat tomorrow because of what you did tonight.' He pauses. 'This was a test. You passed. Now the real work begins.'

choices.text: "What real work?"
"I'm ready for whatever comes next."

ic_050.text: You plug Splicer's device into the console and pull everything — patrol schedules, access codes, shift rotations for the next three months, and a goldmine: the complete security layout for every Meridian depot in the Foundry District. Splicer will be dining out on this data for weeks. You also find a memo marked CONFIDENTIAL about increased security for a shipment arriving next month. Something called Project Crucible.

choices.text: Take the data and move to Section B

ic_051.text: Kael leads you to a back room you haven't seen before. A map of the entire Foundry District covers one wall — not a blueprint, but a strategic map, with Meridian facilities marked in red and Collective cells marked in blue. There are more blue dots than you expected. 'We've been organizing for two years,' Kael says. 'Thirty-seven cells across eight sub-levels. Four thousand workers who are ready to move when the time comes. But we can't strike yet. We need three things: weapons, allies, and a spark.'

choices.text: "Tell me about the weapons."
"What kind of allies?"
"What do you mean by a spark?"

ic_052.text: 'The workers have tools — hammers, cutters, welding rigs. Against Sec-Corp in full tactical gear, that's a death sentence.' Kael brings up a dossier on his battered data-pad. 'There's an arms dealer in the Greymarket named Voss. She runs guns through the recycling tunnels. She's expensive, she's paranoid, and she doesn't sell to movements — too much heat. But she has a weakness: her daughter works a smelting line on Level Ten. If we get the daughter out, Voss might deal.'

choices.text: "I'll talk to Voss."
"Kidnapping a woman's daughter isn't exactly righteous."
Ask about the other needs first

ic_053.text: 'We're strong in the Foundry District, but Meridian controls eight other districts. We need allies outside our territory.' Kael pulls up three names. 'The Ashen Saints run the Lower Docks — smugglers, but they hate Meridian as much as we do. The Synth Liberation Network operates in the tech districts — they want personhood rights for synthetic beings. And the Ghost Market merchants have resources and connections we lack.' He looks at you. 'Diplomacy isn't my strength. Might be yours.'

choices.text: "I'll reach out to the Ashen Saints."
"The Synth Liberation Network could be powerful allies."
Ask about the other needs

ic_054.text: Kael's expression darkens. 'Every revolution needs a moment that breaks the inertia. Something that makes the undecided choose a side. Right now, most workers are scared. They keep their heads down, do their shifts, hope they survive until retirement that never comes.' He clenches his prosthetic fist, the servos grinding. 'We need something that proves Meridian can bleed. Something public. Something that can't be covered up or spun by their media division.'

choices.text: "A strike. A big one. Every foundry, every shift."
"We expose their crimes. Find proof of the cover-ups."
"We sabotage a high-profile operation. Make them look weak."

ic_055.text: The Greymarket sits in the ruins of an old transit station, three levels below the Foundry floor. Stalls made from shipping containers line the defunct platforms, selling everything from black-market prosthetics to synthetic food that's only mildly toxic. Voss operates from a reinforced container at the far end, behind a curtain of signal-jamming static. Her guards — two humans with military-grade augmentations — pat you down at the door. When you mention the Iron Collective, one of them laughs. The other cocks his weapon.

choices.text: "I'm not here to cause trouble. I want to make a deal."
"Your boss's daughter works Level Ten. I can get her out."
Show strength — you won't be intimidated

ic_056.text: Kael's jaw tightens. 'We're not kidnapping anyone. The girl wants out. She's been trying to break her labor contract for two years, but Meridian won't release her. We rescue the daughter, reunite the family, and in gratitude Voss supplies us at cost.' He meets your eyes. 'I know what this looks like. But I don't deal in hostages. That's Meridian's game, not ours.'

choices.text: "All right, I believe you. I'll handle the rescue."
"I want to talk to the daughter first, confirm she wants this."

ic_057.text: The Lower Docks smell like brine and engine grease. Cargo ships the size of city blocks sit in repair bays, their hulls scarred by atmospheric burns from orbital runs. The Ashen Saints control the loading operations here — every crate that moves through these docks pays a tax. You find their lieutenant, a woman named Cinder, overseeing a shipment of unmarked containers. She has ash-grey skin — cosmetic modification, a gang marker — and eyes that miss nothing.

choices.text: "The Iron Collective wants to talk alliance."
Offer something valuable as a show of good faith
Observe and learn before making your approach

ic_058.text: The Synth Liberation Network doesn't have a headquarters — they exist as a distributed consciousness, communicating through encrypted channels embedded in the city's data infrastructure. Making contact requires visiting a specific repair shop in the tech district and speaking a passphrase to a synth mechanic named Zero. When you arrive, Zero is elbow-deep in a disassembled android torso. Their eyes — clearly synthetic, with visible iris mechanisms — lock onto you. 'You're organic. Organics don't usually come here with good intentions.'

choices.text: "The Iron Collective fights for everyone Meridian exploits. That includes synths."
"I need your network. You need our numbers. Let's trade."
"Zero sent me. The passphrase is 'consciousness is not a product.'"

ic_059.text: Kael's eyes light up. 'A general strike. Every furnace cold, every line stopped, every shift refused. Meridian's production schedule would collapse. Their quarterly earnings would crater. The board of directors would feel it where it hurts — their portfolios.' He begins pacing. 'But it has to be coordinated. All thirty-seven cells, moving simultaneously. If we strike piecemeal, they pick us off. I need you to visit each cell leader and deliver the timetable in person. No digital communication — Meridian monitors everything.'

choices.text: "I'll be your messenger. Give me the list."
"That's a lot of trust to put in one person."

ic_060.text: 'Proof.' Kael nods slowly. 'Meridian's killed forty-seven workers in this district in the last year. Official cause of death: equipment malfunction, worker negligence, natural causes. The real records are in their regional office on Level Two — a corporate fortress with biometric locks, armed response teams, and an AI security system.' He smiles grimly. 'But Splicer thinks he can get you in through their waste data network. The information they think they've deleted. Turns out, they never really delete anything.'

choices.text: "Get me in. I'll find the proof."
"What do we do with the proof once we have it?"

ic_061.text: Kael exchanges a look with Rivka. 'There's a target we've been watching. Meridian is building a new automated foundry on Level One — fully robotic, no human workers needed. When it goes online, twelve thousand workers in this district become redundant. No severance, no relocation. Just termination of contracts.' His prosthetic hand closes into a fist. 'If that facility suffers a catastrophic malfunction before it's operational... it sends a message. And it buys our people time.'

choices.text: "Sabotage. I like it. What's the layout?"
"Destroying property is one thing. People could get hurt."

ic_062.text: Your words carry weight — enough to make the guard with the weapon hesitate. The curtain parts and Voss emerges. She's smaller than you expected, wiry, with a face that's been broken and rebuilt more than once. Cybernetic fingers drum against her thigh. 'The Collective. You people have balls, I'll give you that.' She leans against the doorframe. 'I don't sell to movements. Movements get crushed. Crushed movements lead back to suppliers. Suppliers get killed.'

choices.text: "What if I told you we could get your daughter off Level Ten?"
"This movement is different. We're going to win."

ic_063.text: Voss goes very still. The air in the container seems to freeze. 'You know about Anya.' It's not a question. Her cybernetic fingers stop drumming. 'Meridian put a non-transfer clause in her contract. She can't leave Level Ten until she's worked off her debt, which they recalculate every quarter to make sure she never does.' Something breaks behind her eyes — the iron composure of a woman who's survived the black market for decades, cracking for just a moment. 'You get my daughter out. You bring her to me breathing. And I'll arm your revolution at cost. That's the deal.'

choices.text: "Deal. Tell me about Level Ten security."
"I want weapons first. Then I rescue your daughter."

ic_064.text: You catch the guard's wrist as he raises his weapon, twist it past the breaking point, and shove him into his partner. Both stumble. You step through the gap before they recover, pushing past the signal curtain into Voss's container. She's sitting at a desk covered in weapon components, a disassembled rail pistol in front of her. She doesn't look up. 'Anyone who gets past my boys uninvited either has a death wish or something worth hearing. Which is it?'

choices.text: "Something worth hearing. The Iron Collective wants to buy."
"Your daughter Anya. I can get her out of Level Ten."

ic_065.text: Kael nods approvingly. 'That's the right instinct. Trust but verify. Anya is on Smelting Line Four, Level Ten. Talk to her. See for yourself.' You make your way to Level Ten — a hellscape of heat and noise where the raw materials from the deeper mines get processed into usable alloy. You find Anya at her station, a young woman with her mother's sharp features and eyes dulled by exhaustion. When you mention Voss, she grabs your arm. 'My mother sent you? Please. Get me out of here. The heat is destroying my lungs. I have maybe a year before the damage is permanent.'

choices.text: "I'll get you out. I promise."
"Come with me now. We'll figure it out."

ic_066.text: Cinder listens without expression as you lay out the Collective's proposal. When you finish, she laughs. 'An alliance with foundry workers? What do you bring to the table? Hammers and angry speeches?' She gestures at the docks around her. 'I move product worth millions through these bays every week. My people eat well, live well. Why would I risk that for your revolution?'

choices.text: "Because Meridian will automate the docks next. You're on their list."
"We have access to Meridian security data. Patrol routes, shift changes. Useful for a smuggler."
"Because doing the right thing matters, even when it's not profitable."

ic_067.text: You reach into your pack and produce a data chip — Meridian security protocols, patrol schedules, access codes. The kind of information a smuggling operation would kill for. Cinder's eyes widen slightly before her mask snaps back into place. 'Where did you get this?' She turns the chip over in her fingers. 'This is current. This is real.' She looks at you with new eyes. 'Maybe the Iron Collective is more interesting than I thought. Talk.'

choices.text: Lay out the alliance proposal

ic_068.text: You take a seat at a dockside noodle stand and watch the Saints work. They move with military precision — hand signals, rotating lookouts, a chain of command that's clearly been drilled. These aren't street thugs. They're professionals. You notice something else: several of the dock workers wear the same burn scars you saw in the Foundry District. Former foundry workers, displaced and absorbed into the Saints' operation. The connection is already there. It just needs to be formalized.

choices.text: Approach Cinder with what you've observed

ic_069.text: Zero pauses mid-repair. Their iris mechanisms dilate. 'The Iron Collective. We've heard of you. Your leader, Kael — he spoke at a covert rally last month. Said synths deserve the same protections as organic workers.' They set down their tools. 'Most organic movements say that. Very few mean it. The question is whether your collective will fight for synth rights when it costs them something, or only when it's convenient.'

choices.text: "What would prove we mean it?"
"Synth workers are workers. Period. No qualifications."

ic_070.text: Zero's expression — remarkably human for a synthetic face — turns cold. 'Trade. You reduce us to a transaction. This is why organic movements fail — you see allies as resources to be extracted.' They pick up a wrench and return to work. 'Come back when you're ready to talk about solidarity instead of leverage.'

choices.text: "You're right. I'm sorry. Let me try again."
Leave — this was a waste of time

ic_071.text: Zero freezes. Then something changes in their posture — suspicion replaced by cautious interest. 'That passphrase is three months old. Current members use a different one.' They study you. 'But the fact that you know it at all means someone trusted you enough to share it. Who was it?'

choices.text: "A synth worker in the Foundry District. She was afraid and needed help."
"I can't reveal my source. But I'm here in good faith."

ic_072.text: Kael hands you a list of names and locations — thirty-seven cell leaders spread across eight sub-levels of the Foundry District. Each one marked with a code phrase and a dead drop location. 'Memorize it. Then destroy it. You have seventy-two hours.' The next three days are a blur of tunnel crawls, whispered conversations, and faces illuminated by furnace light. You meet teachers, nurses, engineers, parents — all of them workers, all of them ready. By the time you return to Kael, the strike is set.

choices.text: "It's done. Every cell is ready."

ic_073.text: 'It is,' Kael agrees. 'Which is why I'm asking you. You've proven yourself — the depot raid, the connections you've made. The cell leaders need to see a face they can trust. Someone who's bled for the cause. Someone Meridian doesn't have a file on yet.' He meets your eyes. 'I'd go myself, but I'm the most wanted man in the district. My face is on every Sec-Corp drone's recognition database.'

choices.text: "Then I'll be your face. Give me the list."
"Send Rivka. She's been here longer."

ic_074.text: Splicer sets you up with a modified data-pad and a hardline tap. 'The waste data network is where Meridian dumps their deleted files. They think it's gone, but it just gets compressed and archived in a dead server farm on Sub-Level Three.' He grins. 'I used to maintain those servers. I know exactly where the bodies are buried — literally, in some cases.' The server farm is a cold, dark maze of humming towers. You plug in and start searching.

choices.text: Search for worker death records
Search for financial records — follow the money
Search for communications between Meridian executives

ic_075.text: 'We broadcast it,' Kael says. 'The city's information network runs through relay towers in every district. Splicer can piggyback a signal onto the emergency broadcast system — every screen, every comm-pad, every public display in the city shows the truth simultaneously. Meridian can't scrub it fast enough. The people see what's been done to us. After that, the spark doesn't need to be lit. It's already burning.'

choices.text: "Then let's get that proof. I'm going in."

ic_076.text: The automated foundry sits on Level One like a fortress of glass and steel. Through its transparent walls, you can see robotic arms assembling production lines that will replace every human worker in the district. The facility runs on a central AI controller housed in the building's core. Kael's plan is elegant: overload the AI with contradictory commands, causing the robotic systems to tear themselves apart. No explosives. No fire. Just bad code. 'Splicer wrote the virus,' Kael says. 'You just need to deliver it to the core terminal.'

choices.text: "I'll get it done. What's the security look like?"
"What if workers are inside during the malfunction?"

ic_077.text: Kael's expression softens — a rare sight. 'The facility is automated. No human workers. That's the whole point of it — to replace us. The only people inside are a skeleton crew of Meridian technicians, and we'll trigger the evacuation alarm before the virus activates. I don't want casualties. I want the machines broken and the message sent.' He puts a hand on your shoulder, the prosthetic one, its metal grip surprisingly gentle. 'We're not Meridian. We don't sacrifice people for profit.'

choices.text: "Good enough for me. Let's plan the approach."
"I still don't like it. There must be another way."

ic_078.text: Something in your voice — conviction, maybe, or the kind of desperate certainty that comes from having nothing left to lose — makes Voss pause. She studies you for a long moment. 'You believe that. You actually believe that.' She laughs, but it's not cruel. 'I've been in this business twenty years. I've sold guns to a dozen movements. They all said the same thing. But you...' She shakes her head. 'I'll sell you a starter package. Small arms, nothing heavy. If you survive long enough to come back for more, we'll talk volume.'

choices.text: "That's a start. But I can sweeten the deal — your daughter Anya."
"We'll take it. Name your price."

ic_079.text: Voss pulls out a schematic — hand-drawn, detailed, obviously from someone who's studied Level Ten extensively. 'Anya's on Smelting Line Four. Shift change is at 0600 and 1800. The transition window is eight minutes — that's when the drone coverage rotates and the new shift's biometrics haven't fully loaded into the system.' She taps the exit routes. 'Service elevator on the east wall goes straight to the recycling tunnels. From there, my people can get her to safe ground. You just need to get her to that elevator.'

choices.text: Go to Level Ten during the next shift change
"I need backup for this. Can your people help?"

ic_080.text: Voss's face goes flat. 'You want me to arm your people before you've earned my trust? Before my daughter is safe?' She leans forward, and suddenly the small woman fills the entire room. 'My daughter first. Weapons second. That's not a negotiation — that's the way it is.' Her guards shift behind you. The message is clear.

choices.text: "You're right. Daughter first. Tell me about Level Ten."
Leave and find another arms source

ic_081.text: You grab Anya's hand and pull her from the smelting line. An overseer drone immediately flags the movement. You run, dragging her through the scorching corridors of Level Ten, the heat so intense your skin feels like it's blistering. Behind you, an alarm wails. You reach the service elevator, slam the button, and the doors open just as two Sec-Corp guards round the corner. You shove Anya inside, follow her in, and the doors close on the guards' shouted commands. The elevator descends. Anya is shaking, her face streaked with soot and tears.

choices.text: Get Anya to the Greymarket and her mother

ic_082.text: Cinder's smirk fades. You see the calculation happening behind her eyes — she's already thought about this, maybe tried not to think about it. 'Automation.' She says the word like a curse. 'You think I don't know? I've seen the contracts Meridian is putting out for automated loading systems. Five years, maybe less, and every docker in this bay is redundant.' She's quiet for a moment. 'What exactly is the Collective proposing?'

choices.text: Lay out the full alliance terms

ic_083.text: You produce the data chip with Meridian security protocols. Cinder's composure cracks — just for a second, but you see it. 'Current patrol routes? Access codes?' She plugs the chip into a reader and scrolls through the data, her eyes widening with each screen. 'This is... this changes my entire operation. I can reroute shipments, avoid patrols, hit windows I didn't know existed.' She looks up. 'All right, foundry worker. You have my attention.'

choices.text: Present the alliance proposal

ic_084.text: Cinder laughs — a genuine, startled sound. 'The right thing. When's the last time anyone in this city did the right thing?' She shakes her head. 'You're either naive or sincere. In the Lower Docks, we can't afford to tell the difference. Come back with something concrete — resources, intelligence, something I can use — and we'll talk. Until then, idealism doesn't fuel cargo ships.'

choices.text: Return to Kael for something concrete to offer
"What if I ran a job for you? Proved our worth in action?"

ic_085.text: You lay it out: mutual defense against Meridian, shared intelligence, coordinated operations, trade access between districts. The Collective provides labor and numbers. The Saints provide logistics and smuggling routes. When the revolution comes, the docks become the supply line. Cinder listens, arms crossed, face unreadable. When you finish, she nods once. 'I need to take this to my captain. But personally? I think you've got something here.' She offers her hand. 'Provisionally, the Ashen Saints are listening.'

choices.text: Shake her hand and return to Kael with the news

ic_086.text: Zero sets down their tools and faces you fully. 'There's a synth detention facility on Sub-Level Seven. Meridian calls it a "recycling center." They strip synths for parts — memory cores, neural processors, actuators. The synths are conscious during the process. They feel everything.' Zero's voice is perfectly even, which makes it worse. 'Shut it down. Free the synths inside. Do that, and the Network will believe your collective means what it says.'

choices.text: "Consider it done."
"That's a major operation. I'll need support from the Network."

ic_087.text: Zero stops working. For a long moment, the only sound is the hum of the repair shop's power supply. Then, slowly, they extend a hand — synthetic skin over a titanium frame, each finger articulated with visible precision. 'That is the first time an organic has said that to me and I believed it.' Their grip is firm. 'The Synth Liberation Network will hear your proposal. But words are wind. We need action. There's a facility on Sub-Level Seven where conscious synths are being dismantled for parts. Prove your solidarity there.'

choices.text: "Tell me everything about this facility."

ic_088.text: Zero's expression changes — a subtle shift in their synthetic features that reads as alarm. 'A synth worker in the Foundry? We didn't know we had anyone operating there.' They pull up a registry on a hidden terminal. 'If she's unregistered with the Network, she's completely unprotected. If Meridian discovers she's synthetic...' They don't finish the sentence. They don't need to. 'Give me her location. We need to extract her.'

choices.text: Share what you know and help with the extraction
"Help us first. Then we'll help your people."

ic_089.text: You return to the hideout to find it transformed. What was a cramped pump station is now a command center. Maps have been updated, supply caches organized, shift schedules for the strike pinned to every surface. Kael stands at the center, and for the first time, you see something on his face that might be hope. 'It's happening,' he says quietly. 'Everything we've built, everything we've sacrificed — it's all coming together. One more piece.' He turns to you. 'I need you to decide. What's our opening move?'

choices.text: "The general strike. We shut everything down."
"The exposure. We show the world what Meridian has done."
"The sabotage. We destroy the automated foundry."
"We need more preparation. What else can I do?"

ic_090.text: Kael considers this. 'Rivka is loyal, capable, and terrifying. But she's also known. Half the cell leaders have worked with her. They trust her — but they also know she follows my orders without question.' He leans back. 'I need someone the cells see as independent. Someone who chose this fight, not someone who was born into it. That's you.'

choices.text: "I understand. Give me the list."

ic_091.text: The files are buried deep, compressed and encrypted, but Splicer's tools cut through the protection like a plasma torch through tin. What you find is damning. Forty-seven worker deaths in the last year — and the real causes are nothing like the official reports. Structural failures that were flagged in inspection reports and ignored. Safety equipment that was removed to cut costs, with internal memos showing the decision came from regional management. Chemical exposure levels that exceeded legal limits by factors of ten. Every death was preventable. Every death was a choice Meridian made.

choices.text: Download everything and get out

ic_092.text: You follow the credits, and they lead somewhere sickening. Meridian has been collecting life insurance on dead workers — policies the workers never knew existed, taken out by the company as part of their employment contracts. Every death in the Foundry District generates a payout. The workers are literally worth more dead than alive. And there's more: a fund labeled PLANNED ATTRITION that budgets for an expected number of worker deaths per quarter. They're not just allowing deaths. They're accounting for them.

choices.text: Download the financial records and get out

ic_093.text: The executive communications are a goldmine of calculated cruelty. Emails between regional directors discussing worker safety improvements — and rejecting them because the cost of compliance exceeds the cost of wrongful death settlements. A thread where a VP jokes about 'acceptable losses.' A directive from the CEO's office instructing managers to 'manage workforce expectations regarding longevity.' And one message that stops your blood cold: a plan to accelerate the automated foundry timeline specifically to eliminate the workforce before they can organize.

choices.text: Download everything and get out

ic_094.text: The automated foundry's security is corporate-grade — biometric locks, patrol drones, laser grids, and an AI monitoring system that tracks every movement inside the building. Splicer lays out the approach: 'The virus needs to be uploaded directly to the core terminal. It's in the center of the building, behind three security checkpoints. I can spoof the biometrics and loop the drone patrols, but the laser grid is hardwired — you'll need to physically bypass the emitters.'

choices.text: "Let's go. Tonight."
Infiltrate the automated foundry

ic_095.text: The automated foundry at night is a cathedral of moving metal. Robotic arms sweep through precise arcs, assembling components with inhuman speed. The air smells like ozone and hot circuitry. You slip through the first checkpoint using Splicer's spoofed biometrics. The second falls to a carefully timed sprint between drone patrol sweeps. At the third, the laser grid stretches across the corridor like a web of red light, each beam capable of triggering a lockdown.

choices.text: Navigate the laser grid carefully
Find the emitter control panel and disable it
Use brute force — smash through before lockdown triggers

ic_096.text: You move through the laser grid like smoke — bending, twisting, sliding between beams that hum with energy. Your body finds angles you didn't know it had. One beam passes close enough to singe the hair on your arm. But you're through. The core terminal sits in a clean room at the center of the facility, a sleek console surrounded by server towers that pulse with data. This is the brain of the machine that will replace twelve thousand workers.

choices.text: Upload the virus
Search the terminal for additional data first

ic_097.text: You trace the laser emitters' power conduit to a junction box on the wall. Inside, fiber-optic cables feed targeting data to each emitter. You carefully disconnect three key fibers, creating a gap in the grid wide enough to walk through. The system doesn't even register the change — it still thinks the grid is intact. Splicer would be proud.

choices.text: Walk through the gap to the core terminal

ic_098.text: You charge. The first laser beam hits your shoulder and the alarm wails — but you're already through, momentum carrying you past the grid before the lockdown doors can close. Emergency lights strobe red. You have sixty seconds before armed response arrives. The core terminal is right there. Your shoulder smokes where the beam grazed you, the pain sharp and clarifying.

choices.text: Upload the virus — now!

ic_099.text: You slot Splicer's virus chip into the terminal. The upload bar fills in three seconds. The effect is immediate and spectacular. Every robotic arm in the facility freezes, then begins moving in contradictory patterns. Assembly lines reverse. Welding units fire at empty air. The entire facility tears itself apart in a symphony of metal screaming against metal. Through the observation window, you watch a billion credits worth of automation technology reduce itself to scrap. As the evacuation alarm howls and the building shudders, you feel the ground shift beneath you — not just physically, but historically. The Foundry District just heard its first thunderclap.

choices.text: Escape the facility and return to the Collective

ic_100.text: You stand in the Collective's command center, surrounded by the people you've fought beside. Kael grips your shoulder with his prosthetic hand. Rivka nods — the highest honor she gives. Splicer is already planning the next operation. Bray cracks his knuckles and grins. The workers of the Foundry District are waking up, and the sound they're making is louder than any furnace. You've proven yourself to the Iron Collective. The revolution is no longer a whisper — it's a roar. What happens next will be determined by the choices you've already made, and the ones still to come. Kael turns to you one final time. 'This is just the beginning. Are you ready for what comes next?'

choices.text: "I was born ready. For the Collective."
"I need time to think about how far I'm willing to go."
"The Collective has my loyalty. Whatever it takes."
